name: Combinatorial Identities ‚ÄĒ Pascal & Hockey Stick
whatIsIt: Powerful shortcuts for binomial coefficients C(n, k) ('n choose k'). PASCAL'S RULE: C(n, k) = C(n‚ąí1, k‚ąí1) + C(n‚ąí1, k) ‚ÄĒ every entry is the sum of the two above it in Pascal's triangle. ROW SUM: C(n,0) + C(n,1) + ‚Ä¶ + C(n,n) = 2‚ĀŅ. HOCKEY STICK: adding a diagonal, C(r,r) + C(r+1,r) + ‚Ä¶ + C(n,r) = C(n+1, r+1). These turn long sums of combinations into a single value.
whyNeeded: Counting sums that look impossible collapse to one term with these identities. They are the daily bread of Olympiad combinatorics and of proofs by 'double counting'.
realLifeUses: Counting paths on a grid (they follow Pascal's triangle)
Probability sums over all subset sizes

vocabulary.meaning: The number of ways to choose k items from n ('n choose k').
A triangle of binomial coefficients where each is the sum of the two above.
A diagonal sum of coefficients equals a single coefficient below.

story.title: The Triangle's Secret
story.text: Aryan stares at Pascal's triangle. 'How is each number made?' Ma'am points: 'Add the two directly above ‚ÄĒ that's C(n, k) = C(n‚ąí1, k‚ąí1) + C(n‚ąí1, k).' So C(4, 2) = C(3, 1) + C(3, 2) = 3 + 3 = 6. 'And add a whole row: 1 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 8 = 2¬≥.' Aryan grins: 'Every row doubles, and each entry is just its two parents added.'

visual.caption: Row n of Pascal's triangle sums to 2‚ĀŅ, and each entry is the sum of the two above it (Pascal's rule). Here row 3 is 1, 3, 3, 1 ‚ÄĒ total 8.

standardMethod.summary: Recognise the pattern. To split a coefficient, use Pascal's rule (sum of the two above). To sum a whole row, use 2‚ĀŅ. To sum a diagonal of coefficients, use the hockey stick: C(r,r)+‚Ä¶+C(n,r) = C(n+1, r+1).
steps: Identify whether it's an entry, a row sum, or a diagonal sum.
Entry: C(n, k) = C(n‚ąí1, k‚ąí1) + C(n‚ąí1, k).

workedExamples.problem: Use Pascal's rule to find C(4, 2).
Find the sum of row 3 of Pascal's triangle: C(3,0)+C(3,1)+C(3,2)+C(3,3).

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the wrong two entries for Pascal's rule.
commonMistakes.fix: C(n, k) = C(n‚ąí1, k‚ąí1) + C(n‚ąí1, k) ‚ÄĒ the two entries directly ABOVE it.

commonMistakes.mistake: Taking a row's sum as n¬≤ or n instead of 2‚ĀŅ.
commonMistakes.fix: The sum of row n is 2‚ĀŅ (every row doubles the last).

teachBackPrompt: Draw a few rows of Pascal's triangle with a grown-up. Show that each entry is the sum of the two above (C(4,2) = 3 + 3 = 6), that each row sums to 2‚ĀŅ (1+3+3+1 = 8 = 2¬≥), and demonstrate the hockey-stick diagonal 1 + 3 + 6 = 10 = C(5,3).

revisionCard.summary: Pascal's rule: C(n,k) = C(n‚ąí1,k‚ąí1) + C(n‚ąí1,k). Row sum: ő£C(n,k) = 2‚ĀŅ. Hockey stick: C(r,r)+‚Ä¶+C(n,r) = C(n+1,r+1). Each entry = two above; each row doubles. These collapse long sums to one term.

rememberIt.hook: Each entry = two above; each row sums to 2‚ĀŅ
rememberIt.unpack: Pascal C(n,k)=C(n‚ąí1,k‚ąí1)+C(n‚ąí1,k). Hockey stick sums a diagonal to C(n+1,r+1).

studentQuestions.q: Why does a row sum to 2‚ĀŅ?
studentQuestions.a: ő£C(n,k) counts ALL subsets of an n-set (choose any size), and there are 2‚ĀŅ subsets ‚ÄĒ so the row totals 2‚ĀŅ.

studentQuestions.q: Why is it called the hockey stick?
studentQuestions.a: On Pascal's triangle the diagonal you add plus the single answer below form the shape of a hockey stick.

formulas.name: Pascal's rule
formulas.formula: C(n, k) = C(n‚ąí1, k‚ąí1) + C(n‚ąí1, k)
